_work.title: Mesoamerican Divinatory Codices & Calendar Gods
_work.creator: various
_work.date: Late Postclassic – early colonial (c. 1300–1600 CE); some objects earlier
_work.rights: Public domain / CC0 / CC BY-SA (per item). Via Wikimedia Commons.
_work.blurb: The screenfold divinatory books of Postclassic Mesoamerica — the Codex Borgia group (Borgia, Vaticanus B, Cospi, Laud, Fejérváry-Mayer), the Aztec Borbonicus and Codex Ríos, and the Maya Dresden and Madrid codices — together with the gods who govern their 260-day count: Quetzalcoatl, Tezcatlipoca, the death-lord Mictlantecuhtli, the filth-eater Tlazolteotl, and the star-demon tzitzimime. These almanacs map fate, time, the four directions, and the calendar onto a ritual cosmos, crowned by the Aztec Sun Stone.

entries.title: Codex Borgia, Page 20 (Tonalpohualli Almanac)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican / Nahua-Mixtec tlacuilo)
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (facsimile rendering, Chaccard). Underlying codex public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A page from the *Codex Borgia*, the most splendid surviving Mesoamerican divinatory book, named for Cardinal Stefano Borgia and now in the Vatican Library.

## Iconography
The register packs the bar-and-dot numbers and twenty day-sign glyphs of the *tonalpohualli* into compartments, each ruled by a deity figure rendered in the flat, outlined polychrome "Mixteca-Puebla" style. Day signs such as Crocodile (Cipactli), Wind (Ehecatl) and Death (Miquiztli) interlock with patron gods and directional colours.

## Significance
The Borgia is the eponym of the "[[motif:tonalpohualli|Borgia Group]]" of pre-Conquest ritual screenfolds. Its almanacs let a diviner (*tonalpouhqui*) read the fate of a day, a birth, or an undertaking from the 260-day count, cross-tabulated with the nine Lords of the Night and the directional trees. Eduard Seler's monumental commentary (1904–09) remains foundational; Elizabeth Hill Boone has reframed these books as "narratives of fate."

## For artists & game designers
The compartment grid of day-glyph × deity × direction is a ready-made procedural lookup table for a divination minigame.

citations.text: Elizabeth Hill Boone, *Cycles of Time and Meaning in the Mexican Books of Fate* (2007).

entries.title: Codex Borgia, Page 56 (Quetzalcoatl)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican tlacuilo)
entries.summary:
A page from the central "narrative" section of the *Codex Borgia* featuring Quetzalcoatl, the Feathered Serpent.

## Iconography
The Feathered Serpent appears in his Ehecatl-Quetzalcoatl aspect, marked by the buccal mask of the wind god and a conch-shell "wind jewel" (*ehecailacacozcatl*). Plumed serpent forms, ritual paraphernalia and offerings fill the surrounding field in saturated reds, ochres and turquoise.

## Significance
Pages 29–46 of the Borgia form a celebrated cosmological cycle, and Quetzalcoatl recurs throughout as creator, wind, and Venus. As [[motif:quetzalcoatl|Quetzalcoatl]] he is the priest-god of self-sacrifice and the planet Venus; as Ehecatl he sweeps the roads for the rain gods. The ambiguous, much-debated "narrative" of these pages may depict a night-sea journey or a New Fire / Venus-related rite, as argued by Karl Anton Nowotny and later by Boone.

## For artists & game designers
A single deity with stacked aspects — wind, serpent, morning star, culture-hero — is a model for a shapeshifting patron god.

citations.text: Karl Anton Nowotny, *Tlacuilolli: Style and Contents of the Mexican Pictorial Manuscripts* (1961; Eng. 2005).

entries.title: Codex Fejérváry-Mayer, Page 11 (Centeotl)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican tlacuilo)
entries.place: Central Mexico (possibly the Gulf Coast / Mixteca)
entries.summary:
A detail from page 11 of the *Codex Fejérváry-Mayer* showing the maize god Centeotl (Cinteotl), now in the World Museum, Liverpool.

## Iconography
The young maize deity is shown seated, painted with the facial markings and headdress associated with the patron of sustenance, set within a compartment of the divinatory count with its accompanying day-signs and colour fields.

## Significance
The Fejérváry-Mayer is a small, finely painted member of the [[motif:tonalamatl|Borgia Group]], best known for its opening cosmogram of the four directions and the world-tree calendar. Its interior pages, like this one, present *tonalamatl* almanacs assigning patron gods such as Centeotl — god of maize and human sustenance — to segments of the 260-day count, tying agricultural fertility to ritual time.

## For artists & game designers
A crop-god slotted into a calendar wheel ties planting cycles to a fate mechanic — fertility as a turn-based resource.

entries.title: Codex Borbonicus (Complete Screenfold)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Aztec / Nahua tlacuilo)
entries.date: c. 1500–1525 CE (very early colonial / late pre-Conquest)
entries.summary:
A composite reproduction of the complete *Codex Borbonicus*, the great Aztec ritual-calendar screenfold held in the library of the French National Assembly (Palais Bourbon).

## Iconography
The long folded strip opens with a *tonalamatl* of twenty trecenas, each large compartment ruled by patron deities and the Lords of the Day and Night, followed by sections on the 52-year cycle, the New Fire ceremony, and the eighteen monthly (*veintena*) festivals.

## Significance
The Borbonicus is arguably the finest single Aztec manuscript, painted in pure native style on bark paper just before or just after the Conquest. Its [[motif:tonalamatl|tonalamatl]] is the canonical example used to explain how diviners read the 260-day count, and its New Fire scene documents the *toxiuhmolpilia*, the binding of the years every 52 years. Studied by Francisco del Paso y Troncoso and, more recently, Elizabeth Hill Boone.

## For artists & game designers
A single artifact that scrolls from a fate-almanac into a festival calendar is a model for an in-game "living" ritual book.

citations.text: Elizabeth Hill Boone, *Cycles of Time and Meaning in the Mexican Books of Fate* (2007).

entries.title: Codex Vaticanus B (3773), Page 24
entries.creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican tlacuilo)
entries.summary:
A page from the *Codex Vaticanus B* (Vatican Library, Vat. lat. 3773), a pre-Conquest divinatory screenfold of the Borgia Group.

## Iconography
Dense compartments arrange the day-signs, numerical coefficients and presiding deities of the *tonalpohualli*, with directional and color schemes guiding the diviner's reading. The drawing is more abbreviated and schematic than the Borgia's, prioritising calculational clarity.

## Significance
Vaticanus B is one of the five core members of the [[motif:tonalpohualli|Borgia Group]] (with Borgia, Cospi, Laud and Fejérváry-Mayer). Its almanacs are unusually systematic, making it a key witness for reconstructing how Central Mexican diviners combined the 260-day count with the directions, the Lords of the Night, and Venus. Eduard Seler produced the standard early commentary (1902–03).

## For artists & game designers
The terse, calculational layout is a good reference for designing a readable in-game oracle table that still feels arcane.

entries.title: Codex Ríos (Vaticanus A), Day-Sign Malinalli
entries.creator: Anonymous (indigenous painters, colonial Mexico)
entries.place: Mexico / Italy (compiled in New Spain, completed in Italy)
entries.summary:
The day-sign *Malinalli* ("Grass" / twisted grass) as drawn in the *Codex Ríos*, also called *Codex Vaticanus A* (Vatican Library, Vat. lat. 3738).

## Iconography
The twelfth of the twenty day-signs is shown as a stylised skull-and-grass glyph: a death's-head sprouting the curling blades of *malinalli* grass, the emblem of decay turning to new growth.

## Significance
The Ríos codex is an early-colonial encyclopedic manuscript, copied under Dominican supervision (associated with Fray Pedro de los Ríos), that glosses Aztec cosmology, the [[motif:tonalpohualli|day-count]], the heavens and the underworld in Italian. As a glossed copy it is invaluable for decoding pre-Conquest iconography, even as its colonial framing reinterprets it. Malinalli, ruled by Patecatl, was read as an unlucky, death-tinged sign.

## For artists & game designers
A "grass-from-a-skull" glyph is a compact death-and-rebirth icon for a calendar or tarot-style card.

citations.text: Ferdinand Anders & Maarten Jansen, *Religión, costumbres e historia de los antiguos mexicanos* (Codex Vaticanus A facsimile, 1996).

entries.title: Codex Cospi (Bologna Screenfold)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican tlacuilo)
entries.summary:
A view of the *Codex Cospi*, a pre-Conquest divinatory screenfold of the Borgia Group, preserved in the University Library of Bologna.

## Iconography
The deer-hide strip is painted on both sides with *tonalpohualli* almanacs: rows of day-signs and dots framed by directional deities and offerings, with one face given over to a Venus-related and ritual sequence.

## Significance
Cospi (formerly Codex Bologna) is one of the five members of the [[motif:tonalpohualli|Borgia Group]] and one of the very few Mesoamerican books to reach Europe in the sixteenth century, entering the collection of Marchese Ferdinando Cospi in 1665. Its almanacs and Venus pages parallel those of the Borgia and Vaticanus B, helping scholars reconstruct a shared Central Mexican divinatory tradition.

## For artists & game designers
A double-sided ritual strip — fate on one face, a planet-god cycle on the other — is a strong concept for a physical in-game relic.

entries.title: Codex Laud, Folio 43
entries.creator: Anonymous (Central Mexican tlacuilo)
entries.summary:
Folio 43 of the *Codex Laud*, a pre-Conquest divinatory screenfold of the Borgia Group, now in the Bodleian Library, Oxford.

## Iconography
Deity figures and day-signs are arranged in vivid compartments against bands of directional colour; death-and-fertility imagery (skeletal and life-giving gods paired) characterises several of the Laud's almanacs, including marriage and offering prognostications.

## Significance
Named for Archbishop William Laud, who gave it to the Bodleian in the 1630s, the Laud is a core member of the [[motif:tonalpohualli|Borgia Group]]. Its almanacs treat themes such as the fate of marriages and the pairing of death-gods with life-gods, illustrating how the 260-day count structured the most intimate life decisions. The reconstruction of its provenance and content owes much to C. A. Burland and Ferdinand Anders.

## For artists & game designers
Paired death/fertility gods governing marriage omens make an elegant dual-aspect card set for a fate deck.

entries.title: Dresden Codex — Venus Table (Facsimile)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Maya scribes); facsimile by Lacambalam
entries.rights: CC BY-SA 4.0 (facsimile, Lacambalam). Underlying codex public domain. Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A facsimile of the famous Venus pages of the *Dresden Codex*, the finest surviving Maya book, held in the Saxon State Library (SLUB) in Dresden.

## Iconography
Columns of bar-and-dot numbers, Maya glyphs and almanac tables track the 584-day synodic cycle of Venus across its phases, accompanied by deity figures hurling spears — Venus as a malign spear-thrower whose heliacal rising threatened rulers, captives and crops.

## Significance
The Dresden [[motif:venus table|Venus table]] is the most celebrated piece of pre-Columbian astronomy, correlating Venus's appearances with the 260-day *tzolkin* to issue omens. It encodes a strikingly accurate long-term Venus calendar with correction mechanisms. Studied by Ernst Förstemann (who first cracked its numeration in the 1880s), Floyd Lounsbury and others, it proves that Maya divination was inseparable from precise astronomy.

## For artists & game designers
A planet that returns on a fixed clock to deal omen-driven damage is a clean astronomical event mechanic.

citations.text: J. Eric S. Thompson, *A Commentary on the Dresden Codex* (1972).

entries.title: Dresden Codex — Page with the Month Kankin
entries.creator: Anonymous (Maya scribes)
entries.summary:
A detail from the *Dresden Codex* featuring the glyph for the month *Kankin*, part of the Maya calendrical apparatus.

## Iconography
A framed Maya hieroglyphic block names the Haab month Kankin, set among the bar-and-dot numerals and almanac columns by which the codex's scribes laid out divinatory and astronomical tables.

## Significance
The Dresden Codex interweaves the 260-day *tzolkin* and the 365-day *haab* (whose eighteen named "months" include Kankin) into its almanacs and astronomical tables, including the Venus and eclipse cycles. Such month glyphs anchored omens and rituals to specific points in the solar year. The codex's calendrics were decoded by Ernst Förstemann, librarian at Dresden, whose work founded modern Maya epigraphy.

## For artists & game designers
Named "month" sigils that gate which events can occur are a tidy way to give an in-game calendar texture and lore.

citations.text: Michael D. Coe, *Breaking the Maya Code* (1992).

entries.title: The Maya Codices (Madrid / Tro-Cortesianus)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Maya scribes)
entries.summary:
A display of Maya codex pages including the *Madrid Codex* (Codex Tro-Cortesianus), the longest of the surviving Maya books, held in the Museo de América, Madrid.

## Iconography
The screenfold pages carry dense almanacs: rows of *tzolkin* day-glyphs and red-and-black numerals threaded between vignettes of gods sowing, weaving, hunting with snares, and tending hives, each scene tied to favourable or unfavourable days.

## Significance
The Madrid Codex is the great compendium of everyday Maya [[motif:divinatory almanac|divination]]: its almanacs govern agriculture, rain-making, beekeeping, hunting, weaving and disease, mapping the practical concerns of life onto the 260-day count. Reassembled from two fragments (Troano and Cortesianus), it complements the astronomical Dresden Codex by showing divination at the village level. Studied by Victoria and Harvey Bricker, among others.

## For artists & game designers
An almanac of "good and bad days" for each craft is a ready loot/luck system tied to an in-world calendar.

citations.text: Gabrielle Vail & Anthony Aveni (eds.), *The Madrid Codex: New Approaches* (2004).

entries.title: The Aztec Sun Stone (Calendar Stone)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Mexica / Aztec sculptors)
entries.rights: CC0 (photograph, Gary Todd). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
The *Piedra del Sol* (Sun Stone or Calendar Stone), the most famous Aztec monument, a 3.6 m basalt monolith now in the Museo Nacional de Antropología, Mexico City.

## Iconography
A central face — usually identified as the sun god Tonatiuh (or the earth-monster Tlaltecuhtli) — is framed by the glyph *Nahui Ollin* ("4 Movement"), whose four cartouches name the four previous world-ages destroyed by jaguars, wind, fire-rain and flood. A ring of the twenty day-signs encircles the center, bounded by two Fire Serpents (Xiuhcoatl) meeting at the base.

## Significance
The Sun Stone is a cosmogram of the [[motif:five suns|Five Suns]]: the present age, *Nahui Ollin*, is fated to end in earthquakes. It binds the 260-day count, the solar cycle and the myth of cosmic destruction into one carved disk, probably set horizontally as a sacrificial altar or *temalacatl*. It is the single most reproduced emblem of Aztec religion.

## For artists & game designers
A wheel that encodes both a calendar and a prophecy of the world's end is an iconic doomsday-clock motif.

citations.text: Khristaan D. Villela & Mary Ellen Miller (eds.), *The Aztec Calendar Stone* (2010).

entries.title: Mictlantecuhtli, Aztec Lord of the Dead (Ceramic)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Mexica / Aztec sculptors)
entries.rights: CC0 (photograph, Gary Todd). Via Wikimedia Commons.
entries.summary:
A ceramic figure of Mictlantecuhtli, the Aztec lord of the underworld, of the type recovered from the House of the Eagles at the Templo Mayor, Tenochtitlan.

## Iconography
The god stands as a partly skeletal, fleshless figure with a grinning skull, clawed hands, and a liver or other organ spilling from the rib cage — the liver being the seat of the *ihíyotl* soul, drawn to the underworld. Curly hair set with paper rosettes and starry eyes complete the death-lord regalia.

## Significance
[[motif:mictlantecuhtli|Mictlantecuhtli]] ruled Mictlan, the lowest of nine underworlds, where the ordinary dead journeyed for four years. He governs the day-sign Itzcuintli (Dog) and one of the trecenas, and in the *Codex Borgia* he appears back-to-back with Quetzalcoatl in a cosmic life/death pairing. He embodies the Aztec conviction that death and regeneration are inseparable.

## For artists & game designers
A death-god with an exposed liver — the soul-organ — is a vivid, anatomically specific design for an underworld boss.

citations.text: Leonardo López Luján, *The Offerings of the Templo Mayor of Tenochtitlan* (1994).

entries.title: Tlazolteotl, Eater of Filth (Codex Borbonicus)
entries.creator: Anonymous (Aztec / Nahua tlacuilo)
entries.date: c. 1500–1525 CE (very early colonial / late pre-Conquest)
entries.summary:
The goddess Tlazolteotl as depicted in the *Codex Borbonicus*, patroness of one of the twenty trecenas of the divinatory count.

## Iconography
The goddess wears the unspun-cotton headband and spindle-whorl headdress of the spinners and weavers, with black mouth-paint marking her as an "eater of filth." She is shown in the regalia of the *tonalamatl* compartment she governs.

## Significance
[[motif:tlazolteotl|Tlazolteotl]] ("filth deity"), also called Tlaelcuani, "eater of filth," both incited and absolved sexual transgression: she devoured human misdeeds, and a once-in-a-lifetime confession (*neyolmelahualiztli*) to her priests could wipe a person's offenses clean. Goddess of weaving, childbirth, lust and purification, she ruled the trecena beginning 1 Deer and the thirteenth day-sign, binding moral pollution to the diviner's calendar.

## For artists & game designers
A deity who is both the source of sin and its one-time absolution is a rich quest-giver for a confession or atonement mechanic.

citations.text: H. B. Nicholson, "Religion in Pre-Hispanic Central Mexico," *Handbook of Middle American Indians* vol. 10 (1971).

entries.title: Tzitzimitl, Aztec Star Demon
entries.creator: Anonymous (indigenous painter, colonial Mexico)
entries.summary:
A *tzitzimitl*, one of the Aztec star-demons, drawn in a colonial-period manuscript of the type of the *Codex Magliabechiano*.

## Iconography
The figure is a fearsome skeletal female with clawed hands and feet, a skirt of crossed bones, and severed-hand and heart ornaments. Such star-demons are shown frontally in an aggressive, descending posture.

## Significance
The [[motif:tzitzimitl|tzitzimime]] were stars personified as monstrous beings, associated with the goddess group around Cihuacoatl and Itzpapalotl. They were feared above all during solar eclipses and at the close of the 52-year cycle, when, if the New Fire failed to kindle, they were believed to descend and devour humankind. They thus express the Aztec dread of cosmic darkness and the precariousness of the world-age — the danger the New Fire ceremony was meant to avert.

## For artists & game designers
Sky-monsters that descend only during eclipses or a failed ritual make a perfect timed apocalyptic threat.

citations.text: Cecelia F. Klein, "The Devil and the Skirt: An Iconographic Inquiry into the Pre-Hispanic Nature of the Tzitzimime," *Ancient Mesoamerica* 11 (2000).
